PhD Research Proposal

FedOral-3D: A Federated Multi-Agent Autonomous Edge Artificial Intelligence Framework for Preventive Oral Lesion Risk Stratification in Rural India

Mutturaj S Baradol  ·  Senior Project & Program Manager | Aspiring Researcher

Federated Artificial Intelligence Edge Computer Vision Rural Healthcare Intelligence

Research Focus: Preventive Healthcare Intelligence — Not Autonomous Disease Diagnosis

1. Problem Statement

Oral cancer is the most prevalent cancer in India and the third most common cancer overall. Over 70% of cases are diagnosed at Stage III or Stage IV, drastically reducing survival rates. Rural India bears the highest burden — yet faces a severe shortage of oral health specialists, unreliable internet connectivity, and limited diagnostic infrastructure.

Early detection at the community level through risk stratification — not diagnosis — can significantly improve outcomes. Existing Artificial Intelligence solutions, however, are largely designed for urban clinical settings, depend on centralized cloud infrastructure, and do not address the privacy, connectivity, and resource constraints unique to rural Primary Health Centres in India. FedOral-3D proposes a federated, edge-native approach specifically designed for this underserved context.

1.1 Risk Factors in Rural Populations
  • Tobacco consumption and smoking
  • Betel nut chewing
  • Alcohol exposure
  • Poor oral hygiene and nutritional imbalance
  • Chronic inflammatory conditions
1.2 Limitations in Rural Healthcare
  • Shortage of oral healthcare specialists
  • Limited screening infrastructure
  • Poor healthcare digitization and connectivity limitations
  • Delayed specialist referrals and lack of longitudinal monitoring
2. Oral Lesions & Mucosal Abnormalities — Clinical Context

Oral lesions present across a spectrum of mucosal abnormalities. Understanding lesion types is fundamental to building an accurate risk stratification model.

Lesion TypeDescriptionClinical Examples
White LesionsWhite tissue patches or thickened regionsLeukoplakia
Red LesionsRed inflammatory tissue abnormalitiesErythroplakia
Mixed LesionsCombined red-white tissue abnormalitiesErythroleukoplakia
Ulcerative LesionsSurface tissue breakdownAphthous Ulcers
Fibrotic LesionsHardened or thickened tissueOral Submucous Fibrosis
Pigmented LesionsDark or discoloured tissue regionsMelanotic Macule
Inflammatory LesionsSwelling or irritation-related abnormalitiesStomatitis
Surface Texture AbnormalitiesRough or altered tissue structuresKeratosis

FedOral-3D targets early identification of high-risk lesion types — particularly Leukoplakia, Erythroplakia, Erythroleukoplakia, and Oral Submucous Fibrosis — which carry the highest malignant transformation potential and are most prevalent in rural tobacco and areca nut consuming populations.

Clinical Reference Images
Multi-modal Endoscopic Views
(a) Standard white light · (b) Narrow Band Imaging · (c) Enhanced texture mapping · (d) Vascular pattern analysis — illustrating why multi-modal imaging captures what 2D photography misses
Subsurface Vascular Pattern Detection
Narrow Band Imaging reveals irregular vascular architecture (circled) invisible to standard 2D photography — a critical early malignant transformation indicator that FedOral-3D's multi-modal pipeline is designed to capture
Oral Submucous Fibrosis — Clinical Measurement
Reduced mouth opening measured by calibrated gauge — a hallmark of Oral Submucous Fibrosis, highly prevalent in betel nut consuming rural populations and a primary FedOral-3D target lesion with high malignant transformation potential
Urban Clinical Imaging Infrastructure
High-end hospital-grade endoscopy systems — unavailable in rural Primary Health Centres. FedOral-3D is designed to bring equivalent diagnostic intelligence to edge devices used by Accredited Social Health Activists in the field
3. Why Early Preventive Intervention is Critical
3.1 The Prevention Imperative — Key Mortality Insights
  • Over 5 deaths per hour in India alone — oral cancer claims more than one life every 12 minutes
  • The 5-year survival rate has remained stagnant at approximately 32% in India and 50% globally for over 40 years
  • More than 60–70% of oral cancers are diagnosed at Stage III or Stage IV — when treatment is complex and significantly less effective
  • Rural populations have even lower screening access, compounding late-stage detection rates
  • Oral cancer accounts for 40% of all cancers in India — the highest proportion globally
  • India accounts for approximately 28% of global oral cancer deaths despite having only 18% of the world population
  • Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average projections show India's Age-Standardised Incidence Rate rising to 10.15 per 100,000 by 2031 without targeted intervention
  • FedOral-3D directly targets this gap — early detection at the rural screening level
3.2 Oral Cancer Death Rates — India Context

Statistics drawn from PubMed-indexed research and Clarivate Web of Science journals.

Death Rate IndicatorFigureSource
India deaths per year~52,000PubMed: 39357385 — Global Burden of Disease India 2021, Oral Oncology
Deaths per hour in IndiaMore than 5 per hourNational Oral Cancer Registry India
India 5-year survival rate37.2%PubMed: 40198071 — Journal of the American Medical Association Oncology, 10 Indian Population Based Cancer Registries
Rural India 5-year survival34.1%PubMed: 40198071 — Rural versus urban survival disparity
India mortality rate rise+11.18% Annual Percentage ChangePubMed: 39357385 — 1990–2021 annual percentage change
Patients at Stage IV on arrival50.18%PubMed: PMC10911663 — Tata Memorial Hospital Mumbai
Betel nut exposure 5-year survival46% versus 86%PubMed: PMC6965138 — 40-point gap from betel and tobacco use
Screening mortality reduction81% reductionPubMed: 36599217 — 15-year Kerala Randomised Controlled Trial, Journal of Cancer Policy
Oral cancer share of India's cancers40% of all cancersNational Oral Cancer Registry India — Highest proportion globally
2031 India incidence projection10.15 per 100,000PubMed: 39357385 — Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average forecast without intervention
Target publication journalImpact Factor 3.9 · Quartile 1Clarivate Web of Science — Oral Oncology journal
3.3 Why This Research Area is Important ?
52,000Indians die yearly from oral cancer — one death every 10 minutes
34.1%Rural 5-year survival — significantly lower than urban areas due to access gap
50%Arrive at Stage IV — preventive screening before Stage III directly saves lives
40%Survival gap — betel nut and tobacco users survive at 46% versus 86% for non-users

FedOral-3D is designed precisely for this gap — rural, early-stage, preventive intelligence.

4. Research Gap

Existing approaches suffer from three critical limitations:

01
Centralized Artificial Intelligence Models

Require continuous cloud connectivity — infeasible in rural Primary Health Centres where internet access is unreliable or entirely absent. Cloud-dependent systems cannot function during network outages, making them unsuitable for deployment at community health level in remote geographies.

02
Single-Modal Imaging

Misses multi-dimensional lesion characteristics — existing systems rely predominantly on standard 2D oral photography, which captures only surface colour and visible texture. This approach fails to detect subsurface tissue changes, lesion depth, vascular patterns beneath the mucosal surface, and three-dimensional morphological variations that are critical early indicators of malignant transformation. A lesion that appears benign in a 2D photograph may already exhibit significant depth infiltration or irregular subsurface architecture. Multi-modal imaging combining standard photography, depth sensing, and texture analysis provides a substantially richer diagnostic signal — enabling earlier and more accurate risk stratification that 2D imaging alone cannot achieve.

03
Privacy Concerns

Prevent patient data sharing across institutions for model training — traditional centralized machine learning requires aggregating raw patient data in a single location. In the Indian rural healthcare context, this is legally constrained by the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023, ethically problematic given sensitive health data, and practically infeasible given poor connectivity between Primary Health Centres and central servers.

FedOral-3D Addresses All Three
Federated Learning

Trains across distributed hospitals without sharing raw patient data. Model intelligence is built collaboratively while patient data remains on local devices at all times.

Multi-Agent Autonomy

Independent edge agents perform local risk assessment without requiring any central coordination or internet connectivity.

3D Multi-modal Vision

Captures lesion depth, texture, and colour beyond standard 2D photography — providing the multi-dimensional signal necessary for accurate early-stage risk stratification.

Preventive Focus

Risk stratification for early referral, not clinical diagnosis — keeping the framework within appropriate non-clinical boundaries.

6. Expected Contributions
Research Contributions
  • A novel federated multi-agent framework architecture for rural healthcare Artificial Intelligence
  • A privacy-preserving oral lesion risk stratification model
  • A curated multi-modal oral lesion dataset from rural Indian Primary Health Centres
  • Peer-reviewed publications in Healthcare Artificial Intelligence and Medical Imaging
Social Impact
  • Scalable deployment via Accredited Social Health Activists at village level
  • Reduced dependency on specialist availability in remote areas
  • Earlier referrals leading to improved survival outcomes in rural India
  • A replicable framework applicable to other rural preventive screening use cases
